All whose treasures and happiness are laid up on earth will soon be brought to want and misery. It is good to apply what the Scripture says about the vanity and vexation of spirit that attends all things here below to ourselves. Sin has turned the earth upside down; the earth is very different from what it was when God first made it as a place for people to live. At best, it is like a flower which withers in the hands of those who enjoy it and put it in their bosoms. The world we live in is a world of disappointment, a vale of tears; the children of men are few days and full of trouble. See the power of God's curse, how it makes all things empty and lays waste every rank and condition. Sin brings these calamities upon the earth; it is polluted by the sins of men, therefore it is made desolate by God’s judgments. Carnal joy will soon be at an end, and its result is heaviness. God has many ways to make wine and strong drink bitter to those who love them; physical illness, mental anguish, and ruin of property will make strong drink bitter, and the pleasures of the senses tasteless. Let men learn to mourn for sin and rejoice in God; then no person, no event, can take their joy from them.

There shall be a remnant preserved from the general ruin, and it shall be a devout and pious remnant. These few are dispersed like the gleanings of the olive tree, hidden under the leaves. The Lord knows those who are his; the world does not. When the mirth of carnal worldlings ceases, the joy of the saints is as lively as ever because the covenant of grace, the fountain of their comforts and the foundation of their hopes, never fails. Those who rejoice in the Lord can rejoice in tribulation, and by faith may triumph when all about them are weeping. They encourage their fellow-sufferers to do likewise, even those who are in the furnace of affliction or in low, dark, miry places. In every fire, even the hottest, in every place, even the remotest, let us keep up our good thoughts of God. If none of these trials move us, then we glorify the Lord in the fires.

Believers may be driven into the uttermost parts of the earth, but they are singing, not sighing. Here is terror to sinners; the prophet laments the miseries he saw breaking in like a torrent and the small number of believers. He foresees that sin will abound. The meaning is plain: evil pursues sinners. All these things are unsteady and uncertain. Worldly men think to dwell on earth as in a palace, as in a castle, but it shall be removed like a cottage, like a lodge put up for the night. It shall fall and not rise again; but there shall be new heavens and a new earth, in which righteousness will dwell. Sin is a burden to the whole creation; it is a heavy burden under which it groans now and will sink at last. God will visit those who are proud and cruel. Let us judge nothing before the time, though some shall be visited. No one in this world should be secure, even if their condition is ever so prosperous; nor need anyone despair, even if their condition is ever so deplorable. God will be glorified in all this. But the mystery of Providence is not yet finished. The ruin of the Redeemer’s enemies must make way for his kingdom, and then the Sun of Righteousness will appear in full glory. Happy are those who take warning by the sentence against others; every impenitent sinner will sink under his transgression and rise no more, while believers enjoy everlasting bliss.
